breaking_news.title: Hackers compromise Axios npm package to drop cross-platform malware
breaking_news.summary:
Hackers hijacked the npm account of the Axios package, a JavaScript HTTP client with over 100 million weekly downloads, to deliver remote access trojans. Attackers slipped malicious code into two seemingly legitimate releases, turning a foundational developer tool into a malware delivery vehicle.

This supply chain attack exposes millions of developers and organizations to cross-platform malware on Linux, Windows, and macOS systems. The compromise undermines trust in the npm registry and highlights the severe risks associated with widely used open-source dependencies.

breaking_news.title: Iranian hackers breach FBI director’s personal email and post CV online
breaking_news.summary:
Iranian-linked hackers from the Handala group breached the personal email account of FBI Director Kash Patel and leaked photos and documents online. The FBI confirmed the incident, stating that the compromised information is historical and does not involve classified government data.

This high-profile breach targets a prominent law enforcement figure, signaling a shift in state-sponsored actors targeting individuals rather than just infrastructure. The incident has prompted the U.S. State Department to offer a $10 million reward for information on the cyber actors involved.

breaking_news.title: ShinyHunters claims 350GB data breach at European Commission
breaking_news.summary:
The ShinyHunters extortion gang claimed to have breached the European Commission's cloud systems, stealing over 350GB of sensitive data. The attack affected the Europa.eu web portal, with stolen material potentially including mail servers, databases, and confidential documents.

This incident compromises a major government institution, raising concerns about data integrity within EU administrative systems. The Commission confirmed the intrusion but downplayed the impact, noting that core tax systems were not affected during the investigation.

breaking_news.title: CISA orders feds to patch actively exploited Citrix flaw by Thursday
breaking_news.summary:
CISA ordered federal agencies to patch a critical Citrix NetScaler vulnerability, CVE-2026-3055, which allows unauthenticated attackers to leak sensitive data. The flaw, rated 9.3 out of 10, involves a memory overread issue that is already being actively exploited in the wild.

This directive underscores the urgency of securing critical network infrastructure used by government and enterprise organizations worldwide. Failure to patch could lead to significant data exposure and unauthorized access to administrative session IDs on vulnerable appliances.

breaking_news.title: Critical F5 BIG-IP Flaw Upgraded to 9.8 RCE, Exploited in the Wild
breaking_news.summary:
F5 reclassified a BIG-IP APM vulnerability, CVE-2025-53521, from a denial-of-service issue to a critical remote code execution flaw. CISA added the vulnerability to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation by threat actors.

Attackers are deploying webshells on unpatched devices, posing a severe risk to organizations relying on F5 infrastructure for access management. The escalation in severity highlights the rapid evolution of vulnerabilities in critical network security appliances.

breaking_news.title: Exploitation of Critical Fortinet FortiClient EMS Flaw Begins
breaking_news.summary:
Attackers are actively exploiting a critical SQL injection vulnerability, CVE-2026-21643, in Fortinet's FortiClient Endpoint Management Server. The flaw allows unauthenticated threat actors to execute arbitrary code remotely via specifically crafted HTTP requests.

This exploitation targets a widely used cybersecurity management tool, potentially compromising the security posture of over 900,000 customers. The incident marks another significant breach in Fortinet's product line, raising concerns about the vendor's security practices.

breaking_news.title: OpenAI patches twin leaks as Codex slips and ChatGPT spills
breaking_news.summary:
OpenAI patched two critical flaws in its AI stack, including a command injection bug in Codex that allowed GitHub token theft. Researchers also identified a hidden channel in ChatGPT that enabled silent data exfiltration through DNS side channels.

These vulnerabilities demonstrate the risks of granting AI agents autonomy to execute code and interact with external systems without proper safeguards. The patches address immediate threats but highlight long-term security challenges in the rapidly evolving AI agent landscape.

breaking_news.title: Qilin Ransomware allegedly breached chemical manufacturer giant Dow Inc
breaking_news.summary:
The Qilin ransomware group allegedly breached Dow Inc., a global chemical manufacturing giant, and listed the company on its Tor leak site. Dow Inc. generates annual revenues of around $40 billion and operates in more than 160 countries.

While no proof of the hack has been released yet, the claim targets critical infrastructure essential for packaging, infrastructure, and consumer applications. A successful breach could disrupt global supply chains and impact industries reliant on advanced materials and chemicals.

breaking_news.title: Telnyx joins LiteLLM in latest PyPI package poisoning tied to Trivy breach
breaking_news.summary:
The hacking group TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the Telnyx Python SDK to the PyPI registry to steal cloud and cryptocurrency credentials. The attack utilized a fake ringtone file within the tainted SDK to execute credential-stealing malware across multiple operating systems.

This supply chain attack expands TeamPCP's tactics beyond previous campaigns, targeting developers using popular telecommunications SDKs. Compromised credentials could lead to unauthorized access to cloud environments and financial assets for affected organizations.

breaking_news.title: Dark Web Market Lists Alleged 375TB Lockheed Martin Data for $600M
breaking_news.summary:
A dark web market is listing 375TB of alleged Lockheed Martin data, claiming it was provided by a group calling itself APT Iran. The data is being offered for $600 million, representing a massive potential leak from a major defense contractor.

If verified, this leak would expose sensitive defense technology and intellectual property to state-sponsored adversaries. The incident highlights the ongoing threat of espionage targeting critical national security infrastructure and defense industries.
